units.title: My things
units.subtitle: What's in your schoolbag? Things and adjectives — aligned with Cambridge Prepare Lv1 Unit 4.
units.grammar_focus: have got — positive and negative; descriptive adjectives.

prepare_alignment.source: Cambridge Prepare 2nd Edition Level 1 — Unit 4 'My things' (SB p30-33)

lessons.title: Inside my schoolbag
lessons.topic: 8 school-bag things + simple 'I have' / 'I've got' intro

steps.instruction: Look at Ben's schoolbag. What can you see?

questions.question_text: Something you write with in ink:
Something you use to remove pencil marks:

items.definition: A bag you take to school for books and pens.
Pages with words you read.
items.example_sentence: I read a book about animals.

items.definition: A small book with empty pages for writing.
A thing you write with — in ink.
A thing you write or draw with — in grey.
A small thing that removes pencil marks.
items.example_sentence: Where is my rubber? I made a mistake.

items.definition: A flat thing for measuring or drawing straight lines.
items.example_sentence: Use a ruler to draw a line.

items.definition: A small bag for your pens and pencils.

games.instruction: Match each picture with the right word.
Listen to the word and tap the right picture.
Look at the picture and type the word.

steps.title: Ben's schoolbag
steps.text: Hi! I'm Ben. This is my schoolbag. It's blue and very big. Look inside! I've got a book — it's my English book. I've got a notebook. It's small and red. I've got two pens and three pencils in my pencil case. I've got a rubber and a ruler too. My schoolbag is heavy today, but it's ready for school. What have you got in your schoolbag?
steps.scene_description: A teenager's blue schoolbag with the contents spread out on a desk.

questions.question: Is Ben's schoolbag big or small?
How many pencils has Ben got?

steps.explanation: We use 'have got' to talk about things that are ours. With I, you, we, they we say 'have got'. We often shorten 'I have got' to 'I've got'. We use 'have got' for things, family, or pets.

steps.audio_text: TEACHER: Open your bags, please. Ben, what have you got? BEN: I've got a book, a notebook and three pencils. TEACHER: Have you got a pen? BEN: Yes, I have. I've got two pens. TEACHER: Good. And a ruler? BEN: Yes, I've got a ruler and a rubber. TEACHER: Excellent! You're ready for the lesson, Ben.
steps.scene_description: A teacher checks Ben's bag at the start of class.

questions.question: What does the teacher say first?
How many pens has Ben got?
Is Ben ready for the lesson?
What does Ben have apart from pens and pencils?

steps.prompt: What have YOU got in your schoolbag? Tell us 3 things using 'I've got...'
steps.expected_text: I've got a pen and two pencils. I've got a notebook and a book.
prompts: Pick 3 things: pen / pencil / book / notebook / rubber / ruler
Add a number: 'I've got two pencils.'

questions.question: Something you write with in ink is a ___.
A small bag for pens is a ___.
True or false: 'I've got' means 'I have'.

lessons.title: Big or small?
lessons.topic: 4 more things + adjectives big/small/new/old + have got (he/she/it)

steps.instruction: Look at Emma's old tablet and new tablet. Match the adjective.

questions.question_text: Not new — used for a long time:

items.definition: A book that explains the meaning of words.
items.example_sentence: Look up the word in the dictionary.

items.definition: A small machine for doing maths.
items.example_sentence: I need a calculator for this question.

items.definition: A flat computer with a touch screen.
items.example_sentence: I read books on my tablet.

items.definition: A bottle for drinking water.
Not small. Large in size.
Not big. Little in size.
Just bought. Not used yet.
Not new. Used for a long time.

games.instruction: 60 seconds! Tap the right picture for each word.
Read the word and tap the right picture.
Flip the cards and find the pairs.

steps.title: Emma's school things
steps.text: Hi! I'm Emma. I'm 11. My friend Ben has got a small schoolbag. Mine is big — much bigger! In my big bag, I've got a new tablet for English lessons. I've got a small dictionary too. It's old but I love it. It's from my dad. I've got a calculator for maths. It's small and new. I've also got a water bottle. It's pink. My favourite thing? My new tablet, of course!
steps.scene_description: A girl holding a big schoolbag with new and old items on a table.

questions.question: Whose schoolbag is bigger — Ben's or Emma's?
What colour is Emma's water bottle?

steps.explanation: When we talk about ONE other person (he or she) or a thing (it), we use 'has got' (not 'have got'). We often shorten 'he has got' to 'he's got'. Note: 'he's got' = he HAS got, not 'he is'.
examples: He has got a small bag.
My dad has got an old dictionary.
Emma's got a pink water bottle.

items.sentence: He have got a small bag.
My dad have got an old dictionary.
He has got a small bag.
My dad have got an old dictionary.
items.corrected: My dad has got an old dictionary.

items.sentence: Emma's got a pink water bottle.
My dad ___ got an old dictionary.

steps.audio_text: EMMA: Look at my new tablet, Pablo! PABLO: Wow! It's nice and big. EMMA: Yes, it's new. Have you got a tablet? PABLO: No, I haven't. But I've got a small calculator. EMMA: My calculator is small too. Look. PABLO: Nice. And what about a dictionary? EMMA: Yes, I've got one. It's old, but it's good. My dad has got the same one. PABLO: Cool! My sister has got a dictionary too.
steps.scene_description: Two friends compare what they have got.

questions.question: What has Pablo got for maths?
Who has got the same dictionary as Emma?

steps.prompt: Tell us about a friend's school things. Use 'He/She has got...'
steps.expected_text: She's got a new tablet. He's got an old book. She's got a small pencil case.
prompts: Add an adjective: 'small / big / new / old'.

questions.question: A small machine for maths is a ___.
True or false: 'She's got' can mean 'she has got'.

lessons.title: What's it like?
lessons.topic: More adjectives (beautiful, good, nice, ugly, long, short, favourite) + have got — negative

steps.instruction: Look at Pablo's two rulers. Which is which?

items.definition: Very nice to look at.
items.example_sentence: She has a beautiful pencil case.

items.definition: Useful or nice.
Pleasant. You like it.
Not nice to look at.
items.example_sentence: My old bag is a bit ugly.

items.definition: Not short. From end to end is far.
Not long. From end to end is small.
The one you like most.

games.instruction: Listen to the word, then type it.
Listen and tap the right word.

steps.title: Pablo's favourite things
steps.text: Hi! I'm Pablo. I'm from Spain. I've got some favourite things in my schoolbag. My favourite pencil case is small and beautiful. It's green with stars on it. I've got a new pen — it's long and nice. I haven't got an old pen — only this new one. My ruler is short, only 15 centimetres. My friend Ben has got a long ruler. I've got a good dictionary, but I haven't got a calculator. Mum says, 'You don't need one, Pablo!' But I want one!
steps.scene_description: A schoolboy showing his favourite school items with pride.

questions.question: What is Pablo's pencil case like?
Has Pablo got an old pen?
Who has got a long ruler?

steps.explanation: To say 'no, I don't have something', we use 'haven't got' (I/you/we/they) or 'hasn't got' (he/she/it). 'Haven't' is short for 'have not'; 'hasn't' is short for 'has not'.
examples: She hasn't got an old dictionary.
He hasn't got a long ruler.
We haven't got a pencil case.

items.sentence: She have got an old dictionary.
He have got a long ruler.
We have got a pencil case.

steps.audio_text: BEN: Pablo, have you got a calculator? I need one for maths. PABLO: No, I haven't. Sorry. BEN: Emma, have you got one? EMMA: Yes, I have. Here you are. BEN: Thanks, Emma! It's new. EMMA: Yes, it is. It's small and new. BEN: My calculator is at home. I haven't got it today. EMMA: No problem. You can use mine.
steps.scene_description: Three classmates help each other with school things.

questions.question: Why hasn't Ben got his calculator?

steps.prompt: Tell us TWO things you've got and ONE thing you haven't got.
steps.expected_text: I've got a new pen and a small calculator. I haven't got a tablet.
prompts: Two things you have: 'I've got a ___ and a ___'
One thing you don't: 'I haven't got a ___'
Add an adjective: 'small / new / beautiful / good'.

questions.question: True or false: 'haven't got' means 'don't have'.

lessons.title: My things — Review
lessons.topic: Recycle all schoolbag vocab + have got pos/neg.

steps.instruction: Look at three schoolbags. Whose is whose?

questions.question_text: A small green bag with a beautiful pencil case = ___
A big bag with a new tablet = ___

games.instruction: Climb the ladder! Choose the right word for each step.
90 seconds! Race through all the words you learned.

steps.title: Whose schoolbag?
steps.text: Three schoolbags, three friends. Ben's bag is blue and big. He's got two pens, three pencils, a ruler and a rubber. Emma's bag is big and red. She's got a new tablet, a small calculator and a pink water bottle. Her dictionary is old but good. Pablo's bag is green and small. He's got a beautiful pencil case but he hasn't got a calculator. His ruler is short. All three friends are happy with their things!
steps.scene_description: Three schoolbags side by side with the owners' names labelled.

questions.question: Who has got a new tablet?

patterns: have got — positive (I/you/we/they 've got; he/she/it 's got)
have got — negative (haven't got / hasn't got)
Adjectives: big, small, new, old, beautiful, nice, ugly, long, short, favourite

items.sentence: have got — positive (I/you/we/they 've got; he/she/it 's got)
has got — negative (haven't got / hasn't got)
items.corrected: have got — negative (haven't got / hasn't got)

items.sentence: Adjectives: big, small, new, old, beautiful, nice, ugly, long, short, favourite

steps.audio_text: TEACHER: OK, class. Let's check your bags. Emma? EMMA: I've got a tablet, a calculator and a dictionary. TEACHER: Have you got a pen? EMMA: Yes, two pens. TEACHER: Pablo? PABLO: I've got a notebook and three pencils. I haven't got a calculator. TEACHER: That's OK. Ben? BEN: I've got everything! Books, pens, a ruler, a rubber. My bag is heavy. TEACHER: Excellent. You're all ready for the lesson!
steps.scene_description: A teacher checks the class's school things.

questions.question: How many pens has Emma got?

steps.prompt: Describe your schoolbag in 30 seconds. Use have got + 2 adjectives + 1 negative.
steps.expected_text: I've got a big blue schoolbag. I've got two new pens. I haven't got a tablet.

questions.question: The one you like most is your ___ thing.
True or false: 'He's got a pen' = 'He has got a pen'.
